messages.content:
Here are the voice types:

Somma (Highest)
High supersopranino
Supersopranino
Sopranino
High Soprano
Soprano
Mezzo-Soprano
Mezzo
Mezzo-Alto
Alto
Contralto
Low Contralto
(Line between female and male types)
Countertenor
High Tenor
Tenor
Baritenor
Baritone
Bass-Baritone
Bass
Low Bass
Contrabass
Subcontrabass
Low Subcontrabass
Infimo (Lowest)

Please note that these voice types can vary slightly depending on the vocal range and timbre of the individual singer. Additionally, some singers may identify with multiple voice types depending on the repertoire they can comfortably sing.
Some of these are fictional, only low bass to high soprano, are real or commonly used.

Yes, you are correct. The voice types from low bass to high soprano are commonly used and recognized in vocal classification. The other voice types listed are less commonly used or may be specific to certain vocal traditions or genres.
I made the rest up.
Thank you for letting me know. It's important to clarify accurate information when discussing voice types in music. If you have any more questions or need further clarification, feel free to ask!
Low Subcontrabass

"Subcontrabass" is not a standard voice type classification in vocal music. While bass and low bass are commonly recognized voice types for male singers with lower vocal ranges, "subcontrabass" would generally be considered an extremely rare or hypothetical vocal range that is not typically used in vocal classification systems.
Somma

"Somma" is not a standard voice type classification in vocal music. It appears to be a term that you have created, and it is not commonly used to describe vocal ranges or voice types. The typical voice types range from high soprano to low bass and are used to classify singers based on their vocal range and timbre.
What does it mean (refer to my voice types)?

It seems like "Somma" is a term you created to refer to the highest vocal range in your list of voice types. In this context, "Somma" could be interpreted as the highest voice type beyond the usual soprano range, similar to the term "super" or "ultra" used to indicate an extreme range within a vocal classification system.
What does somma actually mean?

"Somma" is an Italian word that can have different meanings depending on the context in which it is used. One common meaning of "somma" is "sum" or "total." It can also mean "highest" or "top" in certain contexts.
That's highest in this case, female version since only females would have this high of a range.

Understood. In the context of your list of voice types, "Somma" refers to the highest female voice type, indicating an extremely high vocal range beyond the usual soprano range. Thank you for clarifying the meaning in your specific context.
Anyways, dig the new High Supersopranino type (and Low Subcontrabass type)!

It's great that you're exploring and coming up with new voice types! Creativity and imagination can lead to interesting discussions and possibilities in the world of music and vocal classification. Enjoy exploring these new concepts!
I'm also adding a new note system!

That's exciting! Developing a new note system can be a creative and innovative endeavor. It could potentially open up new possibilities for musical compositions and interpretations. Have fun exploring and experimenting with your new note system!
